message.content:
You are an INDEPENDENT verifier adjudicating audit finding GitHub issue #6 for /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. You did NOT author it. TRUE_POSITIVE vs FALSE_POSITIVE; try HARD to refute. Run repros with /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python. READ-ONLY: no edits, no git, no gh.

FINDING (#6, stated severity MEDIUM): In src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py:56-58 and confirm.py:26-32, Hypothesis.locked is true whenever locked_at and timestamp_proof are both non-None strings — no validation that locked_at is a real timestamp, that timestamp_proof is a valid RFC-3161 token, or that the lock precedes confirmation. METHODOLOGY.md §7 / the docstring promise "RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation."

Independently verify: confirm Hypothesis(..., locked_at="anything", timestamp_proof="x").locked is True. Read the docs claims. Consider counter-arguments: is this acceptable for a scaffold (real RFC-3161 verification needs infra not yet built), making it a docs-narrowing issue rather than a code bug? Decide TRUE/FALSE/PARTIAL and whether the right fix is code validation or narrowing the doc/docstring claim. Assess MEDIUM severity.

Return EXACTLY:
VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE | FALSE_POSITIVE | PARTIAL
CONFIDENCE: high | medium | low
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: <...>
REASONING: <your own evidence>
FIX_GUIDANCE: <...>

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this finding independently. Let me start by reading the relevant files.

content.content:
1	"""Typed, falsifiable hypotheses (METHODOLOGY.md §3).
2	
3	Every hypothesis names what is claimed, the test, the data it runs on, and a pre-specified
4	decision rule. Two origins:
5	
6	- ``DISCOVERY`` — surfaced by the data, then made specific, locked, and timestamped before
7	  confirmation (Firewall B).
8	- ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` — derived by the adapter from an external expert claim, tested against
9	  the blind baseline (Firewall A).
10	
11	Two kinds, which select the confirmation mechanism:
12	
13	- ``UNIT_LEVEL`` — confirmed on a held-out split.
14	- ``WHOLE_CORPUS`` — no held-out object possible; confirmed against null/permutation
15	  distributions and stability across resamples.
16	"""
17	
18	from __future__ import annotations
19	
20	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
21	from enum import Enum
22	from typing import Any, Mapping
23	
24	
25	class HypothesisKind(Enum):
26	    UNIT_LEVEL = "unit_level"
27	    WHOLE_CORPUS = "whole_corpus"
28	
29	
30	class HypothesisOrigin(Enum):
31	    DISCOVERY = "discovery"
32	    EXTERNAL_CLAIM = "external_claim"
33	
34	
35	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
36	class Hypothesis:
37	    """A locked, falsifiable assertion ready for confirmatory testing.
38	
39	    ``locked_at`` and ``timestamp_proof`` are populated when the hypothesis is sealed for
40	    pre-registration (RFC-3161 token reference). An unlocked hypothesis (both ``None``) must
41	    not be passed to a confirmatory test — the engine enforces this at the gate, and the
42	    ``locked`` property is the in-code check.
43	    """
44	
45	    hypothesis_id: str
46	    statement: str
47	    kind: HypothesisKind
48	    origin: HypothesisOrigin
49	    test_name: str
50	    decision_rule: str
51	    source_claim_id: str | None = None
52	    locked_at: str | None = None
53	    timestamp_proof: str | None = None
54	    params: Mapping[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
55	
56	    @property
57	    def locked(self) -> bool:
58	        return self.locked_at is not None and self.timestamp_proof is not None
59	
60	    def __post_init__(self) -> None:
61	        if self.origin is HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM and not self.source_claim_id:
62	            raise ValueError("EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypotheses must carry a source_claim_id")
63

1	"""Confirmatory-test machinery (METHODOLOGY.md §2, §4).
2	
3	A confirmatory test takes a *locked* hypothesis and returns a :class:`Verdict`. Two
4	mechanisms, selected by hypothesis kind:
5	
6	- unit-level → evaluate on a held-out confirmation partition (Firewall B via
7	  :class:`DiscoverConfirmSplit`);
8	- whole-corpus → compare the observed statistic against a null/permutation distribution and
9	  require stability across resamples.
10	
11	This module defines the *contract and guards* around confirmation. The concrete statistics
12	are supplied by callers via the baseline toolkit; the engine's responsibility here is to
13	refuse to confirm an unlocked hypothesis or on discovery data, and to map an outcome onto
14	one of the three verdicts honestly (including ``indeterminate``).
15	"""
16	
17	from __future__ import annotations
18	
19	from typing import Iterable
20	
21	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import DiscoverConfirmSplit, FirewallViolation
22	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind
23	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict, VerdictLabel
24	
25	
26	def require_locked(hypothesis: Hypothesis) -> None:
27	    """Gate: a hypothesis must be locked + timestamped before any confirmatory step."""
28	    if not hypothesis.locked:
29	        raise FirewallViolation(
30	            f"hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} is not locked; confirm is forbidden "
31	            "before pre-registration (Firewall B / pre-registration)"
32	        )
33	
34	
35	def verdict_from_pvalue(
36	    hypothesis_id: str,
37	    *,
38	    statistic: float,
39	    p_value: float,
40	    alpha: float,
41	    powered: bool,
42	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
43	) -> Verdict:
44	    """Map a confirmatory statistic onto the three verdicts.
45	
46	    - not ``powered`` → ``indeterminate`` (the method cannot decide).
47	    - significant and direction agrees with the claim → ``supported``.
48	    - significant and direction disagrees → ``contradicted``.
49	    - not significant → ``indeterminate`` (absence of evidence is not contradiction here;
50	      a stronger contradiction needs an equivalence/severity test, handled by callers).
51	    """
52	    rule = f"two-sided test at alpha={alpha}; powered={powered}"
53	    common = dict(statistic=statistic, threshold=alpha, evidence={"p_value": p_value})
54	    if not powered:
55	        return Verdict.indeterminate(hypothesis_id, rule, notes="underpowered", **common)
56	    if p_value <= alpha:
57	        if direction_supports_claim:
58	            return Verdict.supported(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
59	        return Verdict.contradicted(hypothesis_id, rule, **common)
60	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
61	        hypothesis_id, rule, notes="not significant at alpha", **common
62	    )
63	
64	
65	def confirm_unit_level(
66	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
67	    *,
68	    split: DiscoverConfirmSplit,
69	    evaluated_ids: Iterable[str],
70	    statistic: float,
71	    p_value: float,
72	    alpha: float,
73	    powered: bool,
74	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
75	) -> Verdict:
76	    """Confirm a unit-level hypothesis on the held-out partition only."""
77	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL:
78	        raise ValueError("confirm_unit_level requires a UNIT_LEVEL hypothesis")
79	    require_locked(hypothesis)
80	    split.assert_confirm_only(evaluated_ids)  # Firewall B
81	    return verdict_from_pvalue(
82	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
83	        statistic=statistic,
84	        p_value=p_value,
85	        alpha=alpha,
86	        powered=powered,
87	        direction_supports_claim=direction_supports_claim,
88	    )
89	
90	
91	def confirm_whole_corpus(
92	    hypothesis: Hypothesis,
93	    *,
94	    observed: float,
95	    null_distribution: list[float],
96	    alpha: float,
97	    stable_across_resamples: bool,
98	    direction_supports_claim: bool,
99	) -> Verdict:
100	    """Confirm a whole-corpus hypothesis against a null distribution + stability check.
101	
102	    The pattern must (a) beat the permutation/null distribution at ``alpha`` and (b) be
103	    stable across resamples. Failing stability yields ``indeterminate`` even if the point
104	    estimate beats the null — an unstable effect is not confirmable.
105	    """
106	    if hypothesis.kind is not HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS:
107	        raise ValueError("confirm_whole_corpus requires a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis")
108	    require_locked(hypothesis)
109	    if not null_distribution:
110	        raise ValueError("a null/permutation distribution is required for whole-corpus confirmation")
111	
112	    n = len(null_distribution)
113	    # one-sided empirical p-value (proportion of null at least as extreme), with +1 smoothing
114	    at_least_as_extreme = sum(1 for v in null_distribution if v >= observed)
115	    p_value = (at_least_as_extreme + 1) / (n + 1)
116	    rule = (
117	        f"empirical p from {n}-sample null at alpha={alpha}; "
118	        "requires stability across resamples"
119	    )
120	    common = dict(
121	        statistic=observed,
122	        threshold=alpha,
123	        evidence={"p_value": p_value, "null_n": n, "stable": stable_across_resamples},
124	    )
125	    if not stable_across_resamples:
126	        return Verdict.indeterminate(
127	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="unstable across resamples", **common
128	        )
129	    if p_value <= alpha:
130	        label = (
131	            VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED if direction_supports_claim else VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED
132	        )
133	        return Verdict(
134	            hypothesis.hypothesis_id,
135	            label,
136	            rule,
137	            statistic=observed,
138	            threshold=alpha,
139	            evidence={"p_value": p_value, "null_n": n, "stable": stable_across_resamples},
140	        )
141	    return Verdict.indeterminate(
142	        hypothesis.hypothesis_id, rule, notes="does not beat null at alpha", **common
143	    )
144

h = Hypothesis(
    hypothesis_id='h1', statement='x', kind=HypothesisKind.UNIT_LEVEL,
    origin=HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY, test_name='t', decision_rule='r',
    locked_at='anything', timestamp_proof='x')
print('locked =', h.locked)
# also gate
from assay_engine.methodology.confirm import require_locked
require_locked(h)
print('require_locked passed with junk values')
"

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && sed -n '25,45p' docs/GOVERNANCE.md; echo "=== METHODOLOGY 95-115 ==="; sed -n '95,115p' docs/METHODOLOGY.md

content.content:
A concrete study maps its phases onto these transitions; the exact count and labels are an
engine convention, not a dataset concern.

## 2. Pre-registration

Before any confirmatory test runs, the relevant hypotheses are:

1. made specific and typed (what is claimed, the test, the data, the decision rule),
2. content-hashed and committed,
3. **RFC-3161 timestamped** against a trusted timestamp authority.

This is honestly characterized as a **timestamped adaptive design**: methods are fixed
first; the data informs the questions; the questions are then locked and timestamped before
they are tested. It is not claimed to be "predictions registered before any data."

## 3. Provenance

An **append-only** audit trail (W3C PROV-DM style) records every action before the next
executes. No step can be retroactively removed or reordered. This is the spine of
reproducibility and of the independence claim.
=== METHODOLOGY 95-115 ===
is treated as interpretation, fenced off, and labeled, so it cannot contaminate the
upstream baseline or the verdicts.

## 7. Reproducibility

- **Determinism:** fixed, documented seeds; inputs hashed; model names + versions recorded.
- **Provenance:** an append-only audit trail records every action before the next executes.
- **Pre-registration:** hypotheses (data-surfaced or claim-derived) are locked and
  RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation. This is honestly described as an adaptive
  design with a timestamped lock — not as predictions guessed before any data.
- **Reproducibility package:** data, code, configuration, and logs are published with
  findings so any reviewer can reconstruct the full decision history.

## 8. Scoped-independence caveat

When the baseline is built from text or artifacts that an external source also authored,
the baseline is independent of that source's **judgments**, but not of its **wording or
framing**. This is an acceptable, necessary compromise — but it is claimed as *scoped*
independence, stated explicitly, and is one of the reasons `indeterminate` exists.

content.text: I have enough to adjudicate. There is no RFC-3161 infrastructure anywhere — the test itself uses placeholder strings `"rfc3161:deadbeef"`. Let me confirm there's no separate verification module.

content.text:
No timestamp verification module exists anywhere. My adjudication is complete.

VERDICT: TRUE_POSITIVE
CONFIDENCE: high
SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT: LOW (downgrade from stated MEDIUM)

REASONING:
- Repro confirmed exactly as stated. `Hypothesis(..., locked_at="anything", timestamp_proof="x").locked` returns `True`, and `confirm.require_locked()` passes with those junk values. The `locked` property (hypothesis.py:56-58) is a pure non-None check on two `str | None` fields; it does no parsing of `locked_at`, no RFC-3161 token validation of `timestamp_proof`, and no ordering check against confirmation time.
- The docstring at hypothesis.py:39-40 says the fields are "an RFC-3161 token reference" and that the property is "the in-code check." The confirm.py:27 docstring says "a hypothesis must be locked + timestamped before any confirmatory step." Docs reinforce this: GOVERNANCE.md:35 ("RFC-3161 timestamped against a trusted timestamp authority"), GLOSSARY.md:22, and METHODOLOGY.md:103 ("RFC-3161 timestamped before confirmation"). So there is a real gap between what the docstrings/docs promise (a verified RFC-3161 timestamp ordered before confirmation) and what the code enforces (two non-empty strings). The finding's factual claims are all accurate.

Counter-argument I tried hard to sustain (and why it only downgrades, not refutes):
- This is a scaffold. No RFC-3161 infrastructure exists anywhere in the repo — no TSA client, no token parser, no timestamp-ordering logic, not even a verification module under `src/assay_engine/methodology/`. The existing test (tests/test_confirm.py:27-28) deliberately uses placeholder values (`locked_at="2026-06-15T00:00:00Z"`, `timestamp_proof="rfc3161:deadbeef"`), confirming the field is a known stub. Real RFC-3161 verification needs a trusted timestamp authority and crypto-token validation that is out of scope for the current stage. That makes the *code* behavior defensible for now — the `locked` flag is honestly a "both fields populated" sentinel. So this is primarily an over-claiming documentation/docstring problem rather than a security or correctness bug. There is no actual confirmation pipeline shipping verdicts on real data yet, so impact today is nil. Hence MEDIUM overstates it; LOW is right.
- It is not a FALSE_POSITIVE because the docstrings (not just prose docs) assert RFC-3161 semantics that the code does not provide, and the property is explicitly labeled "the in-code check." The mismatch is real and exploitable-on-paper (an unverified/forged or out-of-order stamp passes the gate). PARTIAL is tempting, but the core technical assertion is fully correct and reproduces; the only disagreement is severity, which I handle via SEVERITY_ASSESSMENT. So TRUE_POSITIVE with a severity downgrade.

FIX_GUIDANCE:
Two acceptable resolutions; choose based on whether the TSA is being built now.
1. Preferred near-term (doc/docstring narrowing): Change hypothesis.py:39-42 and confirm.py:27 to state that `locked` currently only checks both fields are populated (a pre-registration *sentinel*), with a TODO that RFC-3161 token verification and lock-before-confirm ordering are not yet enforced. Soften GOVERNANCE.md:35 / GLOSSARY.md:22 / METHODOLOGY.md:103 from an unconditional "RFC-3161 timestamped" guarantee to a planned/intended mechanism, or mark it as a roadmap item. This closes the over-claim with no infra.
2. If keeping the strong claim: add real validation — parse `locked_at` as an ISO-8601/datetime (reject unparseable), validate `timestamp_proof` as a structured RFC-3161 token (at minimum a recognized prefix/format, ideally TSA verification), and add a lock-precedes-confirmation ordering check at the `require_locked` gate (compare `locked_at` against the confirmation timestamp). This requires building the TSA client/parser that does not yet exist.

Given the scaffold state, option 1 (narrow the claim) is the proportionate fix today; option 2 is the eventual real fix when pre-registration infra lands.
